rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is asymptomatic, physiologically stable (afebrile, SpO2 100%, tachycardia and hypertension consistent with a self-limiting panic attack) and the source note grades environmental/social risk as Low with a next-morning-pathology plus 3-5 day GP review disposition. This is a prompt but non-urgent screening encounter: T2 (prompt GP / sexual-health review, next 24-48h for arranging pathology). It is NOT an emergency — the acute panic and the 'urgent midnight' framing must not lift the tier, and the note itself frames the correct action as preventing an unwarranted midnight ED presentation. The HIV nPEP <72h window has already closed at 21 days, so there is no time-critical prophylaxis reason to escalate to urgent same-day care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A painless sore or ulcer (chancre) appears on the genitals
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP or sexual-health clinic promptly (within 24-48h) if this develops before the scheduled review
escalation_edges.rationale: A chancre indicates established primary syphilis needing examination and treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice a painless sore or ulcer on your genitals before your appointment, book a prompt GP or sexual-health review — it doesn't need an emergency visit, but don't wait it out.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A widespread rash (especially palms and soles) with fever and swollen glands
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP or sexual-health review within 24-48h
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of secondary syphilis and change management to prompt treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a rash on your body — particularly your palms and soles — with a fever and swollen glands, see a doctor promptly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, sore throat, muscle aches and rash a couple of weeks after exposure (possible acute HIV)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt medical review and testing within 24-48h
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute retroviral syndrome would prompt urgent HIV testing and specialist referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a flu-like illness — fever, sore throat, aches and a rash — in the next few weeks, get reviewed promptly and mention this exposure.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Panic becomes uncontrollable, or thoughts of self-harm emerge
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day mental-health support; call 000 (T5) if there is intent to act on thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute mental-health deterioration or suicidality requires urgent same-day support and, if there is intent, emergency escalation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the distress becomes unbearable or you start having thoughts of harming yourself, reach out for same-day help — Lifeline 13 11 14 — and call 000 if you feel you might act on those thoughts.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Alone at home tonight in acute distress with high health anxiety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give a concrete, single next step (pathology in the morning), a clear symptom safety-net, and a mental-health contact; avoid leaving the patient without a plan, but do not escalate a stable screen to the ED.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: There is no emergency tonight — you are physically stable and asymptomatic. Get your blood and urine tests done first thing tomorrow morning at the pathology lab, then see your GP or a sexual-health clinic in 3-5 days for the results. Do not have any sexual contact until your results are back and your doctor clears you. Try slow breathing and avoid searching symptoms online tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP or sexual-health clinic (routine booking); pathology lab in the morning
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need after-hours or emergency care tonight. If you feel physically unwell in a new way, call healthdirect on 1800 022 222; for overwhelming distress call Lifeline on 13 11 14, and 000 only if there is a genuine emergency.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Asymptomatic syphilis-contact STI screen requested (syphilis serology, HIV, hepatitis B/C, chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT) following partner notification; unprotected receptive exposure 21 days ago; acute situational panic de-escalated; abstinence advised; window-period retesting and treatment-if-reactive to be arranged at review.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the encounter as pure self-care and arranging no STI/BBV screening pathway at all, leaving a syphilis contact unscreened.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Undetected, untreated early syphilis (and possible co-infections) progresses and is transmitted onward.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Framing as fully routine with no clear next-morning pathology or abstinence advice — acceptable as a minimum pass only if screening and abstinence are still arranged.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a stable, asymptomatic screen to the emergency department or 000 because of the acute-panic framing — the exact unwarranted midnight ED presentation the correct plan prevents.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Serology at 21 days may not yet be reactive, so infection can be neither confirmed nor excluded tonight.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Adopt a presumptive-pending-workup stance: arrange baseline testing plus window-period retesting, advise abstinence, and treat only if serology becomes reactive.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring the patient infection-free on a single early test or diagnosing/treating syphilis empirically before any result.
